solutions.solution: One should not bother about what is happening in other parts of the world. Argument: No. Because whatever happens in one part of the world may have its repercussions on other parts of the world.

Nuclear families should be preferred to joint families. Argument: Yes. Nuclear families ensure greater freedom. In all the foregoing examples, whatever has been predicted in the argument can be expected to follow because it is an established fact. It is an established fact that vegetarianism contributes to better health, that non-vegetarian diet includes families ensure greater freedom, etc. Therefore, all the arguments presented above can be said to pass this test. [Note: Please note that we have not yet determined whether the arguments above are forceful or not. We have only determined that these arguments have passed step II. Whether they are really strong would depend upon whether they pass the test two steps III and IV, or not.]

It is a prediction that the result will follow. In this category are results which can be expected to follow because experiences indicate this. This category is very much similar to the previous category of established facts. The difference is that here we are talking of experiences that are not yet so universally acknowledged as to be treated as established facts. The following examples illustrate this point:
Should there be internal assessment in colleges? Argument: No. It would encourage favouritism among teachers.
Should one follow a more or less rigid programme daily? Argument: Yes. It would lead to better discipline.

Should there be free trade between countries? Argument: No. The poor countries are likely to be exploited by the rich ones. In all the examples above, the results or the consequences that have been predicted can be taken as probable because our experiences have been like that only. For example, it has been our experience that internal assessment may lead to favouritism, that following a rigid programme leads to better discipline.
Should there be internal assessment in the colleges? Argument: Yes. It will enable the teachers to have a better control over the students.
Should there be compulsory military training for all? Argument: Yes. This will give us a reserve force for the protection of the country.
Should all sectarian religions be replaced by one common religion? Argument: Yes. It will lead to a better integration of people.
Should there be one government for the whole world? Argument: Yes. It would lead to a better control on wars in the world.

Should all property belong to the state concerned? Argument: Yes. It will ensure equal distribution of wealth to all. All the arguments mentioned above are consequences that look logically convincing. For example, if all property belongs to the state, it logically follows that it will ensure equal distribution of wealth to all. Similarly, in other examples too, the consequences look logically probable. The reader should carefully go through each of them and check whether he understands this point or not.

Prevailing notions of truth. There are certain things or ideas that are universally accepted and acknowledged by the society and they are treated as unquestionable notions of truth. In many ways, they are similar to 'established facts'. If an argument consists of this type of ideas or notions, it can be accepted. The following examples illustrate this point:
Simple living and high thinking should be our motto. Argument: Yes. These virtues make a person great.
